attributes.stem:
A 32-year-old woman presents to the Emergency Department with light vaginal spotting. She is eight weeks pregnant by her last menstrual period. This is her first pregnancy and she has no significant medical history. She does not take any regular medications. On examination, she appears well with vital signs within normal limits. Her abdomen is soft and non-tender. Speculum examination reveals a closed cervical os with minimal dark blood in the vaginal vault.

A transvaginal ultrasound reveals an intrauterine gestational sac with no visible yolk sac or embryo. The mean sac diameter is measured at 28 mm.
attributes.stemQuestion: Which one of the following best describes the diagnostic criteria for missed miscarriage that need to be interpreted for this patient?
options: Mean sac diameter ≥20 mm and crown-rump length ≥5 mm
Mean sac diameter ≥22 mm and crown-rump length ≥9 mm
Mean sac diameter ≥15 mm and crown-rump length ≥6 mm
Mean sac diameter ≥30 mm and crown-rump length ≥8 mm
Mean sac diameter ≥25 mm and crown-rump length ≥7 mm
attributes.explanation:
This patient's ultrasound findings are diagnostic of a missed miscarriage based on the mean gestational sac diameter (MSD) being greater than 25 mm with no visible embryo. Understanding the diagnostic criteria for missed miscarriage is crucial to avoid false positive diagnoses while also providing timely diagnosis and management.

Mean sac diameter ≥25 mm and crown-rump length ≥7 mm represents the current diagnostic criteria for missed miscarriage. Specifically, missed miscarriage can be diagnosed when either: 1) MSD is ≥25 mm with no visible embryo or yolk sac, or 2) Crown-rump length (CRL) is ≥7 mm with no visible cardiac activity.

Mean sac diameter ≥20 mm and crown-rump length ≥5 mm sets the diagnostic threshold too low, which could lead to false positive diagnoses of miscarriage in viable pregnancies.

Mean sac diameter ≥30 mm and crown-rump length ≥8 mm sets the threshold too high, which could delay diagnosis and management unnecessarily.

Mean sac diameter ≥15 mm and crown-rump length ≥6 mm uses measurements that are too small and could lead to false positive diagnoses.

Mean sac diameter ≥22 mm and crown-rump length ≥9 mm combines inappropriate measurements that do not align with current evidence-based criteria.

If measurements fall below these thresholds, a repeat ultrasound in 7-14 days is recommended to definitively diagnose or exclude miscarriage. These conservative criteria were established to achieve 100% specificity for diagnosis of miscarriage, ensuring no viable pregnancy is inadvertently terminated.
learningPoints: Missed miscarriage is diagnosed when there is no embryonic cardiac activity with crown-rump length ≥7mm on transvaginal ultrasound.
In the absence of an embryo, missed miscarriage is diagnosed when the mean gestational sac diameter is ≥25mm with no yolk sac or embryo.
Serial ultrasounds 7-14 days apart may be needed when measurements fall below these diagnostic thresholds.
references: Mercy Perinatal. Ultrasound criteria for miscarriage [Internet]. Heidelberg (VIC): Mercy Perinatal; [cited 2024 Dec 6]. Available from: https://mercyperinatal.com/images/uploads/resources/Ultrasound-criteria-for-miscarriage.pdf

Queensland Health. Guideline for the early pregnancy loss: diagnosis and management [Internet]. Brisbane (QLD): Queensland Health; [cited 2024 Dec 6]. Available from: https://www.health.qld.gov.au/__data/assets/pdf_file/0033/139947/g-epl.pdf
